title: First Christopher Street Liberation Day march in New York
description: On the first anniversary of the Stonewall uprising, marchers walked from Greenwich Village to Central Park in what became known as the Gay Liberation Day March. It is widely counted as the first pride march.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First Gay-in gathering held in San Francisco
description: San Francisco activists staged the first Gay-in, an open-air gathering modeled on the era's be-ins that put gay community life on public display.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Carl Wittman publishes A Gay Manifesto
description: Activist Carl Wittman's essay A Gay Manifesto circulated in the United States, laying out core arguments of the gay liberation movement.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Britain's first gay rights demonstration at Highbury Fields
description: After a Young Liberals activist was arrested for importuning, protesters gathered at Highbury Fields in London in November 1970. It was the first gay rights march held in the United Kingdom.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Netherlands equalizes the age of consent
description: The Netherlands set the same age of consent for same-sex and heterosexual relations, removing the higher threshold that had applied to homosexual contacts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Austria decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Austria repealed its blanket criminal ban on same-sex relations between adults as part of a penal-law reform, though unequal ancillary provisions remained on the books.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Costa Rica decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Costa Rica removed consensual same-sex relations between adults from its criminal law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Finland decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Finland repealed the criminal prohibition of consensual same-sex relations between adults, though a higher age of consent and a ban on so-called promotion stayed in force for years afterwards.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Colorado and Oregon repeal their sodomy laws
description: The US states of Colorado and Oregon both enacted repeals of their sodomy statutes, ending criminal penalties for consensual same-sex relations between adults in those states.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Idaho reinstates its sodomy law after brief repeal
description: Idaho repealed its sodomy statute in a penal-code overhaul, then restored it after protests from Mormon and Catholic constituencies, putting the criminal ban back in force.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Campaign Against Moral Persecution holds Australia's first public gay gathering
description: CAMP was formally established at a meeting in a Balmain church hall on 6 February 1971, the first public gathering of gay women and men in Australia.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Society Five forms in Melbourne
description: The homosexual rights organization Society Five was founded in Melbourne, giving Victoria an early advocacy group of its own.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Baker and McConnell record the first legal US same-sex marriage
description: Jack Baker and James Michael McConnell married in Minnesota, becoming the first legally married same-sex couple in US history and the earliest same-sex marriage recorded in the civil files of any government.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: US Libertarian Party calls for repeal of sodomy laws
description: The newly formed Libertarian Party demanded the repeal of all victimless-crime statutes, explicitly including the sodomy laws, making it an early US party to take that position.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Frank Kameny runs as first openly gay US congressional candidate
description: Astronomer and activist Frank Kameny stood for the District of Columbia's congressional delegate seat, the first openly gay candidacy for the United States Congress.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: University of Michigan opens first collegiate LGBT programs office
description: The University of Michigan created the Gay Advocate's Office, the first office of its kind at a US college or university.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: UK Gay Liberation Front gains national recognition
description: The British Gay Liberation Front was covered in the national press as a political movement, with weekly meetings drawing 200 to 300 people.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: George Klippert, last Canadian jailed for homosexuality, freed
description: George Klippert, the last man imprisoned in Canada for consensual homosexuality, was released from prison two years after the country decriminalized same-sex relations.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ken Togo stands in Japanese national election
description: Openly gay activist Ken Togo ran for national office in Japan, an early moment of queer electoral visibility in the country.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Betty Berzon comes out at UCLA conference
description: At the UCLA conference The Homosexual in America, Betty Berzon became the first US psychotherapist to publicly identify as gay.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Boys in the Sand premieres in New York
description: The film Boys in the Sand opened in New York City, becoming the first gay adult film to carry credits, get a Variety review, and reach crossover commercial success.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Alice B. Toklas Democratic Club forms in San Francisco
description: San Francisco activists founded the Alice B. Toklas Democratic Club, the first gay Democratic club in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Seattle couple applies for same-sex marriage license
description: Faygele Ben-Miriam and Paul Barwick requested a marriage license in Seattle in September 1971. The refusal led to the 1974 court case Singer v. Hara.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hawaii repeals its sodomy law
description: The US state of Hawaii legalized consensual same-sex relations between adults by repealing its sodomy statute.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Norway decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Norway repealed the penal-code provision criminalizing sex between men, ending the country's prohibition of consensual same-sex relations.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: South Australia introduces consenting-adults defence
description: South Australia added a legal defence for consensual same-sex acts between adults in private, a first step toward full decriminalization in the state.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First US city ordinances protecting gay rights
description: East Lansing and Ann Arbor in Michigan, along with San Francisco, became the first US cities to pass ordinances protecting homosexual residents from discrimination.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sweden becomes first country to allow legal gender change
description: Sweden enacted the world's first law letting transgender people change their legal sex, paired with access to free hormone therapy.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First openly gay delegates address the Democratic Convention
description: Jim Foster of San Francisco and Madeline Davis of Buffalo spoke as the first openly gay and lesbian Democratic Convention delegates, urging a gay-rights plank in the party platform. The same year Davis recorded Stonewall Nation, considered the first gay anthem, and co-taught the first US lesbianism course at the University at Buffalo.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jeanne Manford marches with her son, sparking PFLAG
description: Queens schoolteacher Jeanne Manford joined New York's Christopher Street Liberation Day march alongside her son, activist Morty Manford. The gesture is regarded as the starting point of the straight-ally movement and of PFLAG.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Nancy Wechsler becomes first openly gay US officeholder
description: Nancy Wechsler won a seat on the Ann Arbor City Council for the Human Rights Party and came out as lesbian during her term, making her the first openly gay or lesbian person holding political office in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First custody award to an openly lesbian mother in the US
description: Camille Mitchell was granted custody of her children in a divorce case, the first open lesbian in the United States to win such a ruling, though the judge barred her partner from living in the household.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Freda Smith ordained as first openly lesbian MCC minister
description: Freda Smith became the first openly lesbian minister of the Metropolitan Community Church, and also the denomination's first female minister.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: World's first LGBT synagogue founded in Los Angeles
description: Beth Chayim Chadashim opened in Los Angeles as the world's first LGBT synagogue and the first recognized by the Union for Reform Judaism.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Quakers issue Ithaca Statement on Bisexuality
description: The Committee of Friends on Bisexuality published the Ithaca Statement supporting bisexual people, printed in the Friends Journal and The Advocate. It is cited as the first statement on bisexuality by an American religious assembly.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Twin Peaks Tavern uncovers its windows
description: San Francisco's Twin Peaks Tavern removed its blacked-out windows, becoming the city's first gay bar with clear street-facing glass — patrons no longer hidden from public view.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First book on gay witchcraft published
description: Jack Fritscher's Popular Witchcraft: Straight from the Witch's Mouth appeared, the first book to examine gay Wicca and witchcraft.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: West Germany lowers the male same-sex age of consent to 18
description: A penal reform in West Germany cut the age of consent for sex between men to 18. The threshold for heterosexual relations remained 14, so the law stayed unequal.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Malta decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Malta legalized consensual same-sex relations between adults, removing the criminal prohibition from its law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Australian psychiatrists declare homosexuality no illness
description: On 15 October 1973 the Federal Council of the Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatry stated that homosexuality is not an illness — the first psychiatric body in the world to say so, with effect for Australia.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: New Zealand psychiatry drops illness view of homosexuality
description: The 15 October 1973 declaration of the Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatry's Federal Council, the first of its kind worldwide, also ended the illness classification of homosexuality for New Zealand psychiatry.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: American Psychiatric Association removes homosexuality from DSM
description: The American Psychiatric Association struck homosexuality from its Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-II), a decision resting largely on Evelyn Hooker's research and years of activist pressure.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: PFLAG holds its first formal meeting
description: About 20 people, among them founder Jeanne Manford, her family, and Metropolitan Community Church founder Troy Perry, met on 26 March 1973 at the Metropolitan-Duane Methodist Church in Greenwich Village for PFLAG's first formal gathering.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sally Miller Gearhart gains tenure-track post as open lesbian
description: San Francisco State University hired Sally Miller Gearhart, the first open lesbian in a US tenure-track faculty position. She went on to help build one of the country's earliest women and gender studies programs.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Lavender Country releases first gay country album
description: The band Lavender Country put out its self-titled record, the first known gay-themed album in country music.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ohio's sodomy-law repeal takes effect
description: The repeal of Ohio's sodomy statute came into force, ending criminal penalties for consensual same-sex relations between adults in the state.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Fritz Klein founds the Bisexual Forum in New York
description: Dr. Fritz Klein started the Bisexual Forum in New York City, the first support group for the bisexual community.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: National Socialist League forms in Los Angeles
description: The National Socialist League, a self-described gay Nazi party, was set up in Los Angeles — a fringe organization noted in timeline records of the period.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Chile permits legal gender change after surgery
description: Chile allowed a transgender woman to correct her name and sex on her birth certificate following gender-affirming surgery, making it the second country worldwide to permit a legal gender change.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kathy Kozachenko becomes first openly gay elected American
description: Kathy Kozachenko won a seat on the Ann Arbor City Council in Michigan, the first openly gay candidate elected to any political office in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Elaine Noble elected to the Massachusetts State House
description: Elaine Noble won a Massachusetts State House seat, the second openly gay American elected to public office. Her win prompted Minnesota state legislator Allan Spear to come out in a newspaper interview.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: American Christian Cause launches against gay rights
description: Robert Grant established American Christian Cause to fight what it called the gay agenda, an early organization of the modern Christian-right political movement in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First openly LGBT telephone help line opens in London
description: London gained the first openly LGBT phone help line, offering advice and support to callers. The Brighton Lesbian and Gay Switchboard followed a year later.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Brunswick Four arrested in Toronto
description: Four lesbians known as the Brunswick Four were arrested in Toronto on 5 January 1974 after being ejected from the Brunswick House tavern. The incident galvanized the city's lesbian and gay community.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Lesbian Herstory Archives opens in New York
description: Joan Nestle and Deborah Edel opened the Lesbian Herstory Archives to the public from their New York apartment. It grew into the world's largest collection of materials by and about lesbians.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Angela Morley first openly trans Academy Award nominee
description: Composer Angela Morley received an Academy Award nomination for the adapted score of The Little Prince, shared with Alan Jay Lerner, Frederick Loewe, and Douglas Gamley — the first openly transgender person nominated for an Oscar.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: World's first gay softball league forms in San Francisco
description: San Francisco's Community Softball League started play in 1974 with women's and men's teams, most sponsored by gay bars. Teams competed against each other and the San Francisco Police squad.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
